preamble: The shift from a voiced velar stop [g] (or its fricative variant [ɣ], known as 'Ghayn') to the voiced pharyngeal fricative [ʕ] ('Ayin') is a defining historical merger in the Semitic language family. The articulation point moved backward and downward from the soft palate into the throat.

examples.note: PS *ġ (voiced velar/uvular fricative) → Hebrew ʕ (pharyngeal). Greek retained ‘G’ in ‘Gaza’ (Γάζα), attesting the older pronunciation before the merger to pharyngeal was complete.
Same PS *ġ → Hebrew ʕ merger; cf. Arabic gharb (غرب) preserving the original uvular fricative.
Similar merging of velarized/pharyngealized consonants into the Ayin.
